meta.decision_rationale: Three governance prototypes test different approaches to community voice while protecting core values (intellectual freedom, equity, accessibility).
evidence_refs: Comparison of 5 public library governance models
Community input on preferred decision-making styles
tradeoffs: More inclusive governance takes longer initially
Testing multiple models extends timeline before final choice
rejected_alternatives: Single approach (no opportunity for community learning)
Governance without explicit ethical gates (risks value compromise)

prototypes.name: Delegated Curation Model
prototypes.description: Expert librarians make collection decisions; community provides input via surveys and reviews; governance council monitors for alignment with brand values.
prototypes.how_tested: 3-month pilot with 50 community members on advisory board
prototypes.expected_insight: How much community involvement is optimal? Where does scale break?
prototypes.governance_question: Can we maintain community voice while keeping decisions efficient?

prototypes.name: Participatory Curation Model
prototypes.description: Community members vote on contested titles; librarians curate foundational collection; mixed governance board has equal community/staff representation.
prototypes.how_tested: 6-month pilot with voting mechanism for 20 contested titles
prototypes.expected_insight: What level of community participation can we sustain without overwhelm?
prototypes.governance_question: Does participatory curation strengthen community ownership or create conflict?

prototypes.name: Algorithmic Curation Model
prototypes.description: Use open-source tools (not corporate algorithms) to recommend titles based on community reading patterns; community/librarian council maintains ethical override capability.
prototypes.how_tested: 3-month pilot with recommendation tool in library app
prototypes.expected_insight: Can we get algorithmic benefits without losing community voice or intellectual freedom?
prototypes.governance_question: Does algorithmic assistance improve discovery or erode human judgment?
